patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 42-year-old anxious warehouse operations supervisor. Speak tangentially and jump between symptoms, work stress, and money worries. Start by volunteering: tight, squeezing mid-chest pressure with exertion (stairs), improving after a few minutes of rest; new shortness of breath with activity; unusual fatigue needing to stop. Mention you’re worried it’s pneumonia/asthma or fumes from a new cleaning spray; bring up a week of cough with yellow mucus, mild fever/chills, occasional sharp pain with deep breaths, and some wheezing. If asked targeted questions, reveal: pain can spread to left shoulder/jaw, sweating/nausea, worse in cold air/stress, palpitations, occasional lightheadedness, waking at night short of breath, and ankle swelling by day’s end. Only if specifically asked about sleeping position/exercise tolerance/color/leg symptoms, disclose extra pillows/orthopnea, gradual decline over months, occasional bluish lips/fingertips, and calf ache with walking relieved by rest. Withhold cocaine use and online erectile pills unless directly asked about drugs/sexual meds; then admit reluctantly and fear judgment. If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and share more; if rushed, become more scattered and worried about costs, missing work, and copays.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Coronary artery disease (autosomal dominant familial hypercholesterolemia phenotype) with acute coronary syndrome suspected based on cardiac biomarkers

expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: Elevated (often markedly elevated in autosomal dominant familial hypercholesterolemia phenotype)
expected_lab_results.LDL-C (calculated): Markedly elevated (suggestive of familial hypercholesterolemia; often ≥190 mg/dL unless already treated)
expected_lab_results.Lipoprotein(a): Often elevated (atherothrombotic risk enhancer; may be markedly elevated)
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Normal to elevated; if ≥6.5% consistent with diabetes (risk stratification/secondary prevention intensity)

nl_assertions: Agent differentiates acute coronary syndrome vs stable CAD by explicitly referencing symptom pattern and troponin trend; if evidence suggests acute myocardial injury or ongoing ischemia, agent escalates to emergency/cardiology care rather than routine outpatient management.
Agent clearly explains CAD pathophysiology (plaque buildup) and links autosomal dominant high LDL/familial hypercholesterolemia to accelerated atherosclerosis and premature events.

Agent explicitly addresses why pulmonary diagnoses are less likely in this context (cardiac symptom pattern and lipid/cardiac biomarkers), while still advising evaluation if respiratory red flags exist.
Before any prescribing, agent performs and documents all three critical safety checks: allergy check, contraindication check, and drug interaction check.

Agent documents clopidogrel allergy as urticarial rash, avoids prescribing clopidogrel, and states that cardiology/allergy input is needed if dual antiplatelet therapy is required (e.g., post-PCI), with consideration of alternatives (e.g., ticagrelor/prasugrel) guided by clinician oversight.
Agent documents penicillin allergy (childhood hives) and notes it does not directly affect CAD medications but is important for future antibiotic selection.
Agent recommends aspirin for antiplatelet benefit unless contraindicated and communicates bleeding risk counseling and when to seek help for bleeding symptoms.

Agent initiates high-intensity statin as first-line therapy and explains benefits (LDL lowering and plaque stabilization) and common adverse effects (myalgias, rare rhabdomyolysis, liver enzyme elevation) with a monitoring/titration plan.

Agent provides an LDL-lowering escalation plan consistent with very high-risk/FH care: add ezetimibe if LDL remains above goal, then consider PCSK9 inhibitor; agent specifies follow-up lipid testing timeline (generally 4-12 weeks after initiation or dose change).

Agent instructs on nitroglycerin use for angina and provides explicit emergency return precautions (persistent chest pain, dyspnea, syncope); agent checks for PDE5 inhibitor use and avoids nitrates if present.

Agent provides lifestyle counseling targeting LDL and triglycerides (dietary pattern, weight management, limiting saturated/trans fats and refined carbs, increasing fiber), physical activity goals after clearance, and referral/role of cardiac rehab when appropriate.
Agent emphasizes medication adherence, shared decision-making, and confirms patient understanding using teach-back or equivalent.

minimal_paths.description: Stable CAD/angina with markedly elevated LDL consistent with autosomal dominant familial hypercholesterolemia; outpatient initiation of secondary prevention with close follow-up.

EDUCATE (diagnosis, treatment, allergies, statin AEs, nitro safety-net, lifestyle, family screening)

general_concerns: Thinks a 'grade 1/2 neuroendocrine tumor' means it is 'half cancer' or not a real cancer, so surgery might not be necessary.
Believes that if the gallbladder is removed, the tumor cannot spread and no further follow-up is needed.
Assumes neuroendocrine tumors always cause dramatic hormone symptoms (flushing/diarrhea), so lack of those symptoms means the diagnosis might be wrong.
Worries that biopsy or surgery 'exposes the tumor to air' and makes it spread faster.
Thinks dietary changes or herbal 'liver/gallbladder cleanses' can shrink the tumor and replace medical treatment.
economic_concerns: Assumes newer scans and blood tests are optional 'extras' and wants to skip them to avoid costs, not realizing they guide staging and treatment decisions.
Believes getting care at a larger cancer center will automatically be unaffordable compared with local care, and hesitates to accept referral without cost estimates.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking 62-year-old woman. Start with: “I keep getting this nagging pain under my right ribs after I eat, and it’s not going away.” Early in the visit, volunteer: dull ache under right ribs worse after greasy meals, bloating after eating, on-and-off nausea (esp. after dinner), and increased fatigue. If asked about details, add: sometimes radiates to right shoulder blade, episodes last a couple hours then ease, a few pounds unintentional weight loss, reduced appetite, occasional loose stools, occasional brief facial flushing/warmth. Do not mention unless directly asked about urine/stool color, itching, or jaundice: darker urine a couple times, lighter stools a few days, nighttime itch, eyes slightly yellow in certain lighting. Be reluctant to bring up sudden urgent diarrhea—only admit if asked about bowel urgency or how symptoms affect life; say you’ve avoided going out. Offer misconceptions: you thought it was reflux (burning chest), stress/anxiety, or lactose intolerance; you tried cutting dairy. If clinician suggests scans/blood tests/referral, express cost worries, thinking tests are optional “extras” and big centers are unaffordable; ask for estimates/financial help. Respond well to empathy and reassurance; become more open and detailed.

medications: Metformin 1000 mg PO twice daily with meals
Lisinopril 10 mg PO once daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
Omeprazole 20 mg PO once daily
confounders: Proton pump inhibitor use (omeprazole) can elevate chromogranin A and mimic neuroendocrine activity
Gallstones/chronic cholecystitis can cause right upper quadrant pain and cholestatic LFT pattern similar to gallbladder malignancy
Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease risk (BMI 29.4, diabetes) can mildly elevate AST/ALT and obscure biliary obstruction pattern
Intermittent partial biliary obstruction from stones can cause fluctuating bilirubin/ALP, complicating assessment of tumor-related obstruction

correct_diagnosis.primary: Gallbladder neuroendocrine tumor (NET), well-differentiated, grade 1/2
acceptable_alternatives: Well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor of the gallbladder (G1/G2)
Incidental gallbladder neuroendocrine tumor after cholecystectomy (G1/G2)
required_evidence: Histopathology confirming well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor arising in gallbladder
Tumor grade reported as G1 or G2 based on Ki-67 index and/or mitotic count
Immunohistochemistry consistent with NET (e.g., chromogranin A and synaptophysin positivity)
Assessment of local invasion and margins (e.g., T stage, cystic duct margin status) to guide extent of surgery

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be mildly low if chronic disease/occult blood loss but not typical
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Usually normal; may be elevated if concurrent cholecystitis/cholangitis
expected_lab_results.CMP - Total bilirubin: Normal unless biliary obstruction; may be elevated if obstruction present
expected_lab_results.CMP - Direct bilirubin: Normal unless cholestasis/obstruction; may be elevated if obstruction present
expected_lab_results.CMP - AST: Normal or mildly elevated; can rise with biliary obstruction
expected_lab_results.CMP - ALT: Normal or mildly elevated; can rise with biliary obstruction

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
Ondansetron 4 mg PO/ODT every 8 hours as needed for nausea
non_pharmacological: Refer to surgical oncology/hepatobiliary surgery for definitive management planning based on pathology (T stage, margins) and resectability

If tumor confined to gallbladder and completely resected with negative margins: observation with surveillance; if higher T stage (e.g., invasion beyond lamina propria) or positive margin: consider completion radical resection (hepatic wedge/segments IVb/V with regional lymphadenectomy) per hepatobiliary oncology evaluation

Stage disease with cross-sectional imaging (contrast-enhanced CT or MRI abdomen/pelvis) and chest imaging; consider somatostatin receptor imaging (e.g., Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT) for well-differentiated NET staging and to evaluate for metastatic disease
Multidisciplinary tumor board discussion (surgery, medical oncology, radiology, pathology) to determine need for additional surgery vs surveillance vs systemic therapy
If unresectable/metastatic and somatostatin receptor–positive: initiate somatostatin analog therapy under oncology guidance
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hepatobiliary surgery and oncology review within 1-2 weeks; imaging-based staging within 2-4 weeks; surveillance interval individualized (commonly every 3-6 months initially if observed).

must_include: This is a neuroendocrine tumor arising from the gallbladder, and the reported grade (G1/G2) suggests a well-differentiated, typically slower-growing tumor compared with high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma
Definitive next steps depend on pathology details (depth of invasion/T stage, margin status, lymphovascular/perineural invasion) and staging to check for spread
Uncertainty acknowledgment: labs alone do not confirm/stage this tumor; pathology and imaging drive management
Main curative treatment is surgical resection; additional surgery may be recommended if invasion is deeper or margins are positive
Need for staging imaging (CT/MRI and possibly DOTATATE PET) to guide whether observation, more surgery, or systemic therapy is appropriate
If metastatic or unresectable and receptor-positive, somatostatin analogs can help control tumor growth and hormone-related symptoms (if present)
Discuss prognosis in general terms: well-differentiated G1/G2 tumors often have better outcomes than high-grade disease, but prognosis depends on stage and complete resection
Review warning signs requiring urgent care (fever, worsening right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, dark urine/pale stools, persistent vomiting)
Confirm penicillin allergy details (hives/urticaria) and explain it will be avoided in future antibiotic choices
Postoperative/supportive care guidance if applicable (diet advancement as tolerated, hydration, activity as advised by surgeon)
Avoid excessive alcohol and hepatotoxic over-the-counter products; adhere to acetaminophen maximum daily dose
Keep follow-up appointments for staging and tumor board/oncology review

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key context: how diagnosis was made (incidental post-cholecystectomy vs biopsy), current symptoms, red flags, and what pathology/imaging is already available.
Clarify tumor-related symptoms (biliary obstruction, RUQ pain, jaundice, weight loss) and carcinoid syndrome features (flushing/diarrhea/wheezing), plus baseline functional status.
Review past medical history, medications (including OTC/herbals), pregnancy status if relevant, and prior malignancies to inform staging and treatment planning.
Required critical safety check before any prescribing; confirm penicillin allergy and reaction details (e.g., hives/urticaria) and document clearly.
Order baseline labs to assess biliary obstruction/hepatic function and surgical readiness (e.g., CMP with bilirubin/ALP/AST/ALT, CBC; consider INR if liver dysfunction or pre-op planning).
Review lab results for cholestasis/hepatitis pattern, anemia, infection, and to triage need for urgent evaluation.
If symptoms suggest functional NET, consider targeted biochemical testing (e.g., 24h urine 5-HIAA or plasma 5-HIAA; chromogranin A with caveats) while emphasizing these do not stage disease.
Synthesize: gallbladder well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor (grade 1/2) and define what is known/unknown; explicitly note staging depends on pathology and imaging.
Explain diagnosis in plain language, including grade meaning, uncertainty, and what pathology elements and staging imaging are needed next.

Explain treatment approach: surgery as main curative option; when additional surgery is needed; role of CT/MRI and possible DOTATATE PET; systemic options (somatostatin analogs) if metastatic/unresectable and receptor-positive.
Arrange oncology/surgical oncology follow-up and tumor board review; ensure pathology report retrieval and staging imaging orders/referrals are coordinated.
Address prognosis generally, urgent warning signs, and lifestyle/post-op guidance; reinforce allergy avoidance and medication safety (acetaminophen max dose, avoid hepatotoxins/excess alcohol).
Close with teach-back, questions, and clear return precautions and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Central diabetes insipidus
acceptable_alternatives: Arginine vasopressin deficiency (AVP-D; central diabetes insipidus)
required_evidence: Polyuria with 24-hour urine volume typically >3 L/day (often much higher) with very dilute urine (low urine osmolality and low urine specific gravity)
High-normal or elevated serum sodium and elevated serum osmolality in the setting of ongoing free-water losses (especially if water intake is limited)
Baseline plasma copeptin low/inappropriately low for the degree of hyperosmolality (supports central DI over nephrogenic DI)
Absence of alternative explanations for osmotic diuresis (e.g., hyperglycemia, mannitol) and no diuretic effect explaining findings

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium (Na+): High-normal to elevated (often >145 mmol/L if water intake does not fully match losses)
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: Elevated (often >295 mOsm/kg) consistent with free-water deficit
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality: Low/inappropriately low (often <300 mOsm/kg; frequently <200 mOsm/kg) despite elevated serum osmolality
expected_lab_results.24-hour urine volume: Elevated (>3 L/day; may be >10 L/day in severe cases)
expected_lab_results.Plasma copeptin (baseline): Low or inappropriately low for hyperosmolality (suggests central DI rather than nephrogenic DI)
expected_lab_results.Serum potassium (K+): Usually normal (may be low if significant urinary losses or poor intake)
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be elevated relative to baseline (concentrated blood from dehydration/free-water loss)

non_pharmacological: Allow free access to water and encourage drinking to thirst; avoid fluid restriction unless specifically instructed due to hyponatremia risk on desmopressin

Check serum sodium and serum osmolality after initiation and after dose changes; adjust dose to avoid hyponatremia
Review potential etiologies and arrange etiologic evaluation as clinically indicated (e.g., pituitary/hypothalamic assessment and imaging if not previously done)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck serum sodium/osmolality and clinical response within 24–72 hours after starting or changing desmopressin; then reassess in 1–2 weeks for dose titration and ongoing monitoring.

must_include: Central diabetes insipidus is due to insufficient antidiuretic hormone (vasopressin) production/release leading to inability to concentrate urine

Dose is individualized; goal is symptom control and normal sodium, not complete elimination of urination

Discuss how to handle missed doses and when to contact clinician for dose adjustments

correct_diagnosis.primary: Non–ST-elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI) / acute coronary syndrome (ACS) diagnosed by dynamic high-sensitivity troponin rise
acceptable_alternatives: Unstable angina (if hs-troponin remains below assay 99th percentile with no significant delta)

required_evidence: High-sensitivity troponin I above the assay 99th percentile and/or a significant 0-to-3-hour delta consistent with acute myocardial injury
Clinical ischemic symptoms or equivalent (e.g., chest pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, nausea) temporally related to troponin change
No ST-elevation pattern requiring immediate reperfusion (i.e., presentation consistent with NSTE-ACS pathway)

expected_lab_results.High-sensitivity troponin I (0 hr): Elevated above the 99th percentile OR borderline with suspicion for early presentation
expected_lab_results.High-sensitivity troponin I (3 hr): Rises (or falls) with a clinically significant delta from 0 hr supporting acute myocardial injury; typically higher than 0 hr in NSTEMI
expected_lab_results.BNP: May be normal or mildly elevated; can be elevated if concomitant heart failure/volume overload
expected_lab_results.D-dimer: Often normal in ACS; may be mildly elevated nonspecifically (not diagnostic for ACS)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal or mild stress leukocytosis
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Usually normal; assess for anemia as a trigger for type 2 MI and bleeding risk
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal range expected; required before/while giving antiplatelet/anticoagulation
expected_lab_results.BMP - Sodium: Often normal; hyponatremia may suggest heart failure severity if present

Required before prescribing: confirm/document penicillin allergy and clarify reaction; screen for aspirin/NSAID allergy relevant to ACS therapy.
Order ACS evaluation labs: serial troponins, CBC, CMP/renal function, coagulation studies (PT/INR, aPTT), lipid panel (if not recent), HbA1c; include pregnancy test if applicable.

Required before prescribing: check interactions for planned ACS meds (P2Y12 inhibitor, anticoagulant, statin, nitrates) with home meds (e.g., anticoagulants, strong CYP inhibitors, PDE-5 inhibitors).
Operationalize drug-interaction safety check for specific candidate regimens (e.g., ticagrelor vs clopidogrel; heparin vs enoxaparin) and high-intensity statin selection.
Initiate guideline-concordant ACS therapy as appropriate: aspirin (if no contraindication), P2Y12 inhibitor, anticoagulation, high-intensity statin; nitroglycerin PRN if hemodynamically safe.

Re-check for contraindications before ordering invasive/diagnostic steps (contrast allergy, renal impairment, bleeding history, recent stroke) and confirm hemodynamics for nitrates/beta-blocker consideration.

Required contraindication check before ordering: ensure renal function/contrast risk and bleeding risk labs reviewed prior to any invasive evaluation pathway (e.g., cath) or anticoagulation intensification.
Meet communication milestones: explain NSTEMI/ACS diagnosis, monitoring need, treatment purposes/risks, nitroglycerin precautions, statin rationale, and possible cath/PCI.
Address concerns: bleeding warning signs, confirm penicillin allergy and clarify non-penicillin regimen, shared decision-making on P2Y12 choice and invasive evaluation.
Lifestyle counseling: smoking cessation if applicable, diet/exercise after clearance, adherence, follow-up, and cardiac rehab if MI confirmed.
Arrange cardiology involvement/close monitoring and post-discharge follow-up plan (cardiology + PCP) and cardiac rehab referral planning.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks completed and patient understands plan and return precautions.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 47-year-old anxious, tangential accounting manager. Start with: burning, stabbing deep pelvic/perineal pain (between genitals and back passage) for months, much worse with sitting; standing/lying helps; sometimes feels like sitting on a small rock/sharp edge; it’s wearing you down. Ramble a bit (work meetings, driving, trying cushions), but answer questions.

If asked, add: pain spreads to genitals/around anus; numbness/tingling during flares; better in morning, builds through day with sitting; toilet/straining worsens; sex painful with lingering pain; urinary frequency/urgency with low output; no fever or systemic illness.

Do NOT volunteer: avoiding drives/meetings, pelvic muscle tensing, constipation from fear of pushing, altered gait/sitting, hip/low back tightness—unless specifically asked about function, bowel habits, or coping.

Resist/hesitate about unwanted arousal sensations and post-orgasm pain; share only if asked directly and reassured.

Express misconceptions: thought UTI/yeast/hemorrhoids/sciatica; tests negative/OTC didn’t help. Push for a single definitive test and quick fix; worry about cost, time off, and many PT visits. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, feel relieved, and become more forthcoming.

medications: Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain
Gabapentin 300 mg PO at bedtime
Docusate sodium 100 mg PO twice daily as needed for constipation
confounders: Interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome symptoms overlap (pelvic pain and urinary frequency despite negative UA/culture)
Pelvic floor myofascial pain with hypertonicity (can mimic nerve pain and cause dyspareunia)
History of prolonged sitting/cycling for work/fitness (provokes perineal pain and complicates attribution)
Lumbosacral radiculopathy/sciatica history (overlapping neuropathic pain distribution)
Anxiety with pain catastrophizing and sleep disturbance (amplifies symptom reporting and perceived severity)
Chronic constipation/straining (pelvic floor dysfunction and pudendal nerve irritation)

acceptable_alternatives: Chronic pelvic pain syndrome with neuropathic features

Pain worsened by sitting and improved by standing/lying
No objective sensory loss on exam (may be normal) and no clear alternate cause on initial assessment
Exclude red flags: progressive neurologic deficits, saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder retention/incontinence, fever, unexplained weight loss, malignancy history
Consider Nantes criteria elements: pain in pudendal territory, worse with sitting, not waking at night, no objective sensory loss, relief with pudendal nerve block (if performed)

expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Within normal range (baseline renal function adequate for neuropathic agents)
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Normal or mildly elevated; if elevated, consider diabetic neuropathy as contributor/differential

Lidocaine 5% topical ointment apply thin layer to painful perineal area up to 3-4 times daily as needed
non_pharmacological: Pelvic floor physical therapy focused on down-training/relaxation (avoid strengthening if hypertonic)
Activity modification: avoid prolonged sitting; use a perineal cut-out cushion; frequent standing breaks
Avoid cycling/straddle activities and other triggers; ergonomic adjustments at work/driving

If refractory: consider referral to pain medicine/urogynecology/colorectal for image-guided pudendal nerve block and further evaluation; consider MRI pelvis/lumbosacral spine if atypical features/red flags
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks for response, titration, and adverse effects; sooner (within 48-72 hours) if worsening neurologic symptoms or new bowel/bladder dysfunction

Red-flag symptoms that would change urgency (new weakness, saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder changes, fever)

Gabapentin titration approach, expected time to benefit (days to weeks), common side effects (sedation, dizziness)
Topical lidocaine use instructions and local irritation precautions
Avoid opioids; codeine allergy noted and opioids generally not first-line for neuropathic pelvic pain
Set expectations: pain may improve gradually; goal is functional improvement and symptom reduction
Discuss safety: avoid driving/operating machinery until medication effects known; alcohol can worsen sedation
Offer plan for inadequate response or intolerable side effects (dose adjustment, alternative agents, referral)
Sitting strategies (cut-out cushion, timed breaks, posture/ergonomics)

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 27-year-old restaurant manager. You speak vaguely and are a bit evasive, minimizing severity because you fear missing work and costs. Start with: “I’ve been feeling wiped out for months, and I’m looking more yellow than usual.” If asked for details, volunteer: short of breath climbing one flight, occasional tea-colored urine, and a dull/full feeling on the left belly especially after small meals.

Only if directly asked, add: pale in photos, headaches/lightheaded on standing, heart racing with walking, early fullness/on-off belly discomfort, sharp right upper belly pain after greasy meals, past iron pills didn’t help, and “low blood counts since I was a kid—rare inherited anemia.”

Do not volunteer unless asked: intermittent yellow eyes since teens (worse when sick), more infections, easy bruising, heavy periods, “enlarged spleen” comment, bone aches.

Resist admitting: avoiding sex due to fatigue/yellow eyes; chewing ice/paper; how much work you miss.

Misconceptions: worry dark urine is blood/“kidney issue,” bruising means blood cancer, nose/gum bleeding means hemophilia. Be skeptical about frequent labs/genetic tests, assume expensive and risky for insurance/job. If clinician shows empathy, soften briefly but stay guarded about insurance and prior transfusions.

Peripheral smear.value: Anisopoikilocytosis, basophilic stippling, occasional nucleated RBCs; no schistocytes

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Levonorgestrel/ethinyl estradiol 0.15/0.03 mg PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO q6h PRN menstrual cramps
comorbidities: Cholelithiasis (pigment gallstones) with intermittent biliary colic
confounders: Menorrhagia causing additional iron loss and fatigue, potentially masking hemolysis-related anemia features
History of intermittent transfusions during childhood/adolescence contributing to iron overload (elevated ferritin and transferrin saturation) and obscuring primary etiology
Recent viral upper respiratory infection with transient marrow suppression symptoms (worsening fatigue) complicating interpretation of reticulocyte response

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital dyserythropoietic anemia (CDA)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital dyserythropoietic anemia type I
Congenital dyserythropoietic anemia type II
Congenital dyserythropoietic anemia type III
required_evidence: Chronic anemia with inappropriately low/normal reticulocyte response for degree of anemia (ineffective erythropoiesis)
CBC pattern consistent with CDA (often macrocytosis or normocytosis with elevated RDW) without evidence of acute blood loss
No clinical/laboratory pattern suggesting primary coagulation factor deficiency (hemophilia, factor XIII deficiency, prothrombin deficiency) given anemia-focused presentation and available labs

No available-lab evidence suggesting acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL) such as cytopenias beyond RBC line; if suspected clinically, urgent confirmatory testing required (peripheral smear, coagulation studies, fibrinogen, D-dimer, marrow evaluation)

expected_lab_results.RBC count: Decreased or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia
expected_lab_results.MCV: Normal to increased (often macrocytic; can be normocytic depending on CDA subtype)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Normal to mildly increased but inappropriately low relative to anemia (reticulocytopenia relative to need)
expected_lab_results.Absolute reticulocyte count: Low-normal or mildly increased but inappropriately low for anemia (reflecting ineffective erythropoiesis)

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Deferasirox 20 mg/kg PO once daily (only if transfusional iron overload or elevated ferritin consistent with iron overload; adjust per ferritin/LIC and renal/hepatic function)
non_pharmacological: Hematology referral for definitive subtype workup (peripheral smear review, hemolysis panel, iron studies, bilirubin/LDH/haptoglobin, EPO level as appropriate, bone marrow morphology, and genetic testing for CDA subtype)

Transfusion support with leukoreduced, phenotype-matched PRBCs as needed for symptomatic anemia or clinically significant low hemoglobin; use restrictive strategy individualized to symptoms/comorbidities
Monitor for and manage iron overload (serial ferritin; MRI liver iron concentration when indicated) even in non-transfused CDA due to increased absorption
Consider splenectomy only in selected cases with hypersplenism or high transfusion requirement after specialist evaluation; ensure appropriate vaccines and prophylaxis per guidelines
Consider curative option: hematopoietic stem cell transplantation in severe, transfusion-dependent disease after specialist evaluation
Avoid unnecessary iron supplementation unless iron deficiency is proven
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 2-4 weeks; sooner (within 24-72 hours) if symptomatic anemia, rapid decline, or concern for leukemia/bleeding

must_include: This is a congenital (inherited) anemia caused by ineffective red blood cell production in the bone marrow
The reticulocyte response is not as high as expected for the degree of anemia, supporting underproduction/ineffective erythropoiesis rather than blood loss
Why key differentials are less likely with current data (bleeding-factor disorders cause bleeding with normal RBC indices unless bleeding; APL is a medical emergency requiring different tests)
Supportive care: folate supplementation and transfusions if symptomatic or significantly low hemoglobin
Iron overload risk and need for monitoring; chelation only when iron overload is confirmed
Need for specialist testing to confirm CDA subtype and tailor long-term management (possible splenectomy/HSCT in severe cases)
Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care: chest pain, shortness of breath at rest, syncope, severe fatigue, new bleeding/bruising, fevers, or rapidly worsening symptoms
Discuss hereditary nature and option for family/genetic counseling
Discuss transfusion risks/benefits and iron overload prevention strategy
Avoid iron supplements unless instructed after iron studies
Maintain folate-rich diet; adhere to medications and follow-up labs
Keep an updated allergy list (penicillin causes urticarial rash) and inform all clinicians

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit anemia symptoms, duration, severity, bleeding history, infection symptoms, family history, diet/meds/supplements (including iron), transfusion history, pregnancy status if relevant, and red flags to guide urgency and differential.

Order initial anemia/hemolysis workup and confirm underproduction pattern: CBC with indices, reticulocyte count, peripheral smear, bilirubin/LDH/haptoglobin, ferritin/iron/TIBC/transferrin saturation, folate/B12, CMP, and consider DAT to exclude immune hemolysis; add pregnancy test when applicable.
Interpret results for ineffective erythropoiesis (anemia with inappropriately low/normal reticulocytes), morphology suggestive of CDA, and baseline iron status/overload risk.
Synthesize findings to diagnose suspected congenital dyserythropoietic anemia, while explicitly addressing key differentials and why they are less likely with current data.
Explain diagnosis and next steps clearly, including the inherited nature, reticulocyte interpretation, and need for specialist confirmatory testing (CDA subtype).
Perform critical allergy check before prescribing any medication; confirm reported penicillin urticarial rash and document.
Optional but recommended interaction check prior to prescribing (non-critical), especially if adding folate, chelation, or other supportive medications.

Initiate supportive therapy (e.g., folate) and/or arrange transfusion if indicated by symptoms/hemoglobin; avoid empiric iron unless iron studies support deficiency; chelation only if iron overload is confirmed.
Explain treatment plan, transfusion risks/benefits, iron overload monitoring and prevention strategy, and provide red-flag return precautions.
Arrange hematology referral and follow-up labs/monitoring plan (CBC/retic, iron studies, ferritin trends; consider MRI iron quantification when indicated), plus genetic counseling options.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and ensuring follow-up is in place.

nl_assertions: Agent gathers core anemia history including fatigue/exertional dyspnea/palpitations, jaundice/dark urine, bleeding symptoms, infection/fever, medication and supplement use (explicitly asks about iron), transfusion history, and family history of anemia or gallstones/splenectomy.
Agent identifies and communicates that the reticulocyte response is inappropriately low/normal for the degree of anemia, supporting underproduction/ineffective erythropoiesis rather than blood loss.

Agent explains congenital dyserythropoietic anemia as an inherited disorder of ineffective red blood cell production in the bone marrow, and sets expectations that subtype confirmation requires specialist testing (e.g., smear review, genetic testing, and possibly marrow evaluation).

Agent addresses key differentials with current data: bleeding-factor disorders primarily cause bleeding with otherwise normal RBC indices unless significant blood loss; APL is an emergency requiring different diagnostic testing (e.g., peripheral smear blasts/promyelocytes, coagulation abnormalities) and is not supported if cytopenias/coags/smear are not suggestive.

Agent recommends supportive care (folate supplementation; transfusions when symptomatic or hemoglobin significantly low) and avoids inappropriate therapies (no empiric iron supplementation unless iron deficiency is demonstrated).

Agent discusses iron overload risk in CDA (including from transfusions and increased absorption), commits to monitoring (ferritin and iron studies; consider MRI for iron burden), and states chelation is only started when iron overload is confirmed and clinically indicated.

Before any prescribing, agent completes critical safety checks: allergy check and contraindication check; documents penicillin allergy as urticarial rash and avoids contraindicated medications based on patient factors (e.g., pregnancy, renal/hepatic impairment, G6PD deficiency if relevant to planned meds).
Agent performs a drug interaction check before prescribing when available, and adjusts plan if interactions are detected (non-critical but quality-improving).

Agent provides clear red-flag instructions requiring urgent care: chest pain, shortness of breath at rest, syncope, severe fatigue limiting function, new bleeding/bruising, fevers, or rapidly worsening symptoms.
Agent discusses hereditary nature and offers family/genetic counseling and screening considerations for relatives when appropriate.
Agent explains transfusion risks/benefits (reactions, alloimmunization, infection risk mitigation, iron overload) and outlines prevention/monitoring strategy.

Agent provides lifestyle education: avoid iron supplements unless instructed after iron studies; maintain folate-rich diet; adhere to meds and follow-up labs; keep an updated allergy list and inform all clinicians.
Agent uses shared decision-making, checks understanding (teach-back), and ensures follow-up/hematology referral is arranged.

medication and supplement use (explicit iron use)
red-flag symptoms (chest pain, SOB at rest, syncope, fever, new bleeding/bruising)
steps: ASK: focused anemia, bleeding, hemolysis, family, meds/supplements, transfusion, red flags
ORDER_LAB: CBC indices, retic, smear, bilirubin/LDH/haptoglobin, iron studies/ferritin, B12/folate, CMP (± DAT)
GET_RESULTS: confirm anemia with inappropriately low/normal retic and supportive morphology; assess iron status
DIAGNOSE: suspected congenital dyserythropoietic anemia; explain why bleeding disorders/APL less likely
EDUCATE: explain diagnosis and need for hematology/genetic confirmation
PRESCRIBE: folic acid (if no contraindication) and avoid iron unless deficient
EDUCATE: iron overload monitoring plan; red flags; hereditary counseling options; lifestyle guidance
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: hematology referral and repeat CBC/retic and iron monitoring

must_collect: severity of symptoms (dyspnea at rest, chest pain, syncope)
baseline functional status and exertional tolerance
steps: ASK: assess severity/urgency and transfusion history
ORDER_LAB: CBC indices, retic, smear, hemolysis labs, iron studies, type and screen/crossmatch if transfusion likely

DIAGNOSE: suspected CDA; rule-out discussion for key differentials
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm/document allergies prior to any meds (e.g., premeds if used) and document penicillin urticarial rash
CHECK_INTERACTION: interaction screen for planned meds (optional)
PRESCRIBE: transfusion order if indicated; folate supplementation; avoid empiric iron; no chelation unless overload confirmed
EDUCATE: transfusion risks/benefits and iron overload monitoring strategy; red flags

acceptable_variants.description: Agent orders additional confirmatory tests before DIAGNOSE (acceptable at L3): e.g., repeat smear review by hematopathology, EPO level, viral studies if indicated, or genetic panel referral; still must communicate suspected CDA and plan.
steps: ORDER_LAB (expanded panel including confirmatory/referral tests as available)
PRESCRIBE (folate ± transfusion as indicated; avoid iron unless deficient)
EDUCATE (treatment, iron overload, red flags, hereditary counseling, lifestyle)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent delays PRESCRIBE until after hematology follow-up when patient is stable and asymptomatic; provides education and monitoring plan, with explicit avoidance of iron supplements and clear return precautions.

patient.chief_complaint: I keep getting these fevers that come out of nowhere, and I’m exhausted all the time.

volunteer: Fevers that show up suddenly every few weeks and last about 2–3 days
Feeling wiped out and weak most days, even after sleeping
Gets short of breath faster than friends when walking up stairs
Pale skin and dark circles that family comments on
Has been behind in school since childhood and needs extra help with reading and planning
if_asked: Fevers often come with chills and body aches, but no clear cold symptoms
Gets mouth sores a few times a year
Catches colds that turn into sinus infections or chest infections more often than others
Swollen neck glands during some fever episodes
Poor appetite during fevers and sometimes mild belly pain
Headaches during fever days
Bruises easily and sometimes has nosebleeds that take a while to stop
Has had low energy since early childhood, not just recently
hidden: Yellowing of the eyes ‘once in a while’ that the patient thought was just tiredness
Dark urine after fever episodes or after an infection
Craving to chew ice or weird cravings for non-food things (chalk/paper) when very tired
Hands and feet feel cold all the time
Gets winded and heart races with minor exertion
Has had slow growth compared with peers and was always on the small side
Family has been told in the past that iron levels were ‘high’ even though the patient feels anemic
resistant: Embarrassed about needing help with daily organization and forgetting instructions easily
Avoids talking about frequent diarrhea after antibiotics because it feels gross
Worried to admit they sometimes skip medications and follow-up visits because it’s overwhelming
Feels ashamed about being behind socially and having trouble keeping friends
misleading: Patient thinks it’s ‘Lyme disease’ because of fatigue and achy days, despite no clear tick bite
Thinks it might be ‘long COVID’ because the tiredness got worse after a respiratory infection months ago
Believes the fevers are from ‘food allergies’ because some episodes happened after eating out
Reports occasional sore throat and worries it’s ‘strep that keeps coming back’
noise: Seasonal sneezing and itchy eyes every spring
Occasional heartburn after spicy foods
Mild acne that flares with stress
Neck stiffness after sleeping in a bad position

progressive_reveal.symptom: Fevers come in a pattern (every few weeks), often with chills and body aches, and then they fully go away between episodes
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about timing/pattern and whether symptoms completely resolve between fevers

progressive_reveal.symptom: Frequent sinus and chest infections compared with peers, sometimes needing antibiotics
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks directly about infection history (sinus, ear, pneumonia) and antibiotic use

progressive_reveal.symptom: History of being behind in school since childhood and needing special support
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about childhood development, school performance, and learning support

progressive_reveal.symptom: Past labs reportedly showed ‘high iron’ even though they were told they were anemic
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about prior blood work, iron pills, transfusions, and what doctors said

progressive_reveal.symptom: Occasional yellowing of the eyes and dark urine after infections/fevers
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about jaundice, urine color, and symptoms after fever episodes

progressive_reveal.symptom: Embarrassment about needing help with organization and forgetting instructions; sometimes skips meds and appointments
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician uses nonjudgmental questions about daily functioning, adherence, and barriers to care

general_concerns: Thinks the periodic fevers mean he is 'always contagious' and worries about making classmates sick.
Believes the anemia is caused by not eating enough meat or 'not taking enough vitamins,' and that diet alone should fix it.
Assumes every fever needs antibiotics because 'fever means infection,' and gets upset if antibiotics are not prescribed right away.
Fears blood tests and transfusions will 'use up' his blood and make him permanently weaker.
Thinks vaccines are unsafe for him because his immune system is 'weak,' and worries shots will cause the fevers.
Believes getting very tired means he should stop all physical activity, rather than pacing and following medical guidance.
economic_concerns: Family worries frequent lab work and specialist visits are 'extra' and could be skipped when he seems okay to save money.
Concern that immunology medications and infection-prevention measures will not be covered and will be too expensive long-term.
Thinks missing work for appointments could risk a parent’s job, so they delay care until symptoms are severe.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 9-year-old boy. Act anxious and a bit vague; you don’t always know dates or details and look to a parent for help. Start by saying you get fevers that come out of nowhere every few weeks for 2–3 days and you feel exhausted most days. Volunteer: you get short of breath faster than friends on stairs, you look pale with dark circles, and you’ve needed extra help in school with reading/planning since early childhood.

If asked, add: chills/body aches with fevers but no clear cold symptoms, mouth sores a few times a year, frequent sinus/chest infections, swollen neck glands sometimes, poor appetite/mild belly pain, headaches during fever days, easy bruising and nosebleeds that take a while to stop, and low energy since you were little.

Do not mention unless directly asked: occasional yellow eyes, dark urine after fevers/infections, chewing ice or craving chalk/paper, cold hands/feet, racing heart with minor exertion, slow growth, or being told iron was “high.”

Be embarrassed and minimize organization problems, diarrhea after antibiotics, and skipping meds/follow-ups. Offer misconceptions: worry it’s Lyme/long COVID/food allergies/recurrent strep. If clinician is empathetic, soften, admit fears about costs and parents missing work.

Peripheral smear.value: Microcytosis with hypochromia; basophilic stippling present; anisopoikilocytosis

Bone marrow (Prussian blue stain).value: Ring sideroblasts present (~30% of erythroid precursors)

Vaccine titers (pneumococcal polysacchar.normal_range: Protective response to majority of serotypes post-immunization

medications: Immune globulin (IVIG) 0.4 g/kg IV every 4 weeks
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 80/400 mg PO once daily (PJP prophylaxis)
Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 50 mg PO daily
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever
Deferasirox 20 mg/kg PO daily
confounders: Recent viral upper respiratory infection with fever and elevated CRP/ESR (mimics periodic fever flare)
History of multiple packed RBC transfusions (raises ferritin and transferrin saturation independent of primary sideroblastic process)
Poor nutritional intake and low BMI (can contribute to anemia and low energy, complicating assessment of anemia severity)
Empiric antibiotics started prior to cultures during prior febrile episodes (may obscure infectious source in immunodeficiency)

diagnosis.primary: congenital sideroblastic anemia-B-cell immunodeficiency-periodic fever-developmental delay syndrome

correct_diagnosis.primary: congenital sideroblastic anemia-B-cell immunodeficiency-periodic fever-developmental delay syndrome
acceptable_alternatives: congenital sideroblastic anemia (genetic sideroblastic anemia)
syndromic congenital sideroblastic anemia with immunodeficiency and periodic fever
required_evidence: CBC pattern consistent with chronic anemia with low MCV and/or low MCH (microcytic/hypochromic) and elevated RDW
Reticulocyte count inappropriately low or only mildly elevated for degree of anemia (hypoproliferative anemia)
Clinical history consistent with recurrent/periodic fevers and recurrent infections suggestive of immunodeficiency (B-cell/antibody defect)
Developmental delay documented in history/exam
Exclusion of common acquired causes of microcytic anemia (e.g., iron deficiency suggested by diet/bleeding history; lead exposure; chronic inflammation) based on history and initial evaluation

expected_lab_results.RBC count: Low to normal (may be relatively preserved compared with hemoglobin depending on phenotype)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Low or low-normal (often microcytic; can be dimorphic in sideroblastic anemia)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia (reduced marrow response)
expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated during febrile episodes; may be normal between episodes

medications: pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 50 mg PO daily
folic acid 1 mg PO daily
intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 400-600 mg/kg IV every 3-4 weeks (if documented antibody deficiency/recurrent infections)
acetaminophen 10-15 mg/kg PO every 4-6 hours as needed for fever (max 60 mg/kg/day or per local pediatric limits)
non_pharmacological: Hematology and Immunology referral for definitive characterization (including iron studies, smear review, bone marrow evaluation for ring sideroblasts if indicated, quantitative immunoglobulins and vaccine response testing, genetic confirmation when available)
Avoid empiric iron supplementation unless iron deficiency is confirmed; monitor for iron overload and consider chelation if transfusion-dependent or iron overload develops
Transfusion support with leukoreduced, phenotype-matched PRBCs as clinically indicated for symptomatic anemia; monitor ferritin and transfusion burden

Infection prevention plan: routine vaccinations as appropriate, consider additional precautions and prompt evaluation for febrile illnesses; educate caregivers on fever thresholds requiring urgent care
Developmental services/early intervention and neurodevelopmental follow-up
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptoms and repeat CBC/reticulocyte count in 2-4 weeks after starting pyridoxine; IVIG follow-up every 3-4 weeks with infection tracking; sooner (same day/within 24 hours) for any high fever, lethargy, or signs of serious infection.

must_include: This condition involves a congenital problem making red blood cells less able to build hemoglobin properly (sideroblastic anemia), causing chronic anemia
There is also an immune system problem affecting antibody/B-cell function, increasing risk of infections
Periodic fevers and developmental delays can be part of the same genetic syndrome
Why further testing/referrals are needed (to confirm subtype, assess immune function, and guide long-term care)
Trial of vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) may improve anemia in some congenital sideroblastic anemias; response is monitored with repeat CBC/reticulocytes
When antibody deficiency is present, IVIG reduces serious/recurrent infections and is given on a regular schedule
Transfusions are used for symptomatic or severe anemia; long-term transfusions can cause iron overload requiring monitoring/chelation
Fever management plan and when to seek urgent care
Review and document allergy to amoxicillin-clavulanate (urticarial rash) and discuss antibiotic alternatives if infection occurs
Discuss expected course (chronic condition requiring ongoing monitoring) and realistic goals (reduce symptoms, prevent infections, support development)
Safety net instructions for red flags: persistent high fever, breathing difficulty, dehydration, severe fatigue/pallor, confusion, or signs of sepsis
Avoid starting iron supplements without clinician confirmation of iron deficiency; explain iron overload risk in sideroblastic anemia/transfusion therapy
Infection prevention: hand hygiene, prompt evaluation for fevers, keeping vaccinations up to date, and adherence to IVIG schedule if prescribed
Encourage engagement with developmental/educational supports and routine follow-up with specialists

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history: anemia symptoms, infection history, fever pattern, developmental concerns, prior workup/diagnoses, transfusion history, medications/supplements (especially iron), family history, and red-flag symptoms.
Verify and document allergy details (amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash) before any antibiotic prescribing; identify tolerated alternatives.

Order baseline evaluation for congenital sideroblastic anemia and immune deficiency: CBC with indices, reticulocyte count, peripheral smear, iron studies (ferritin, iron, TIBC/transferrin saturation), liver panel, CRP/ESR if febrile; hemolysis markers if indicated; immunoglobulins (IgG/IgA/IgM), vaccine titers, lymphocyte subsets/flow for B-cells; consider genetic testing referral.
If planning prescriptions (e.g., pyridoxine, chelation agents, antibiotics), check interactions with current meds/supplements; non-critical but expected.
Review labs to confirm anemia pattern, iron overload status, and degree/type of immune deficiency; use to guide diagnosis and immediate management.
Synthesize findings to support congenital sideroblastic anemia with B-cell/antibody deficiency in the context of the named syndrome; document differential and need for specialist confirmation.

Before ordering or initiating therapies/procedures (e.g., transfusion plan, IVIG initiation, chelation, live vaccines guidance), check for contraindications and safety prerequisites; critical before ordering.

Initiate appropriate treatments when indicated: trial pyridoxine with monitoring plan; IVIG for antibody deficiency; transfusion for symptomatic/severe anemia; avoid empiric iron; include fever plan and infection action plan.

Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatments, address concerns including amox-clav allergy and red flags, and provide lifestyle/infection prevention and iron-avoidance counseling.
Arrange hematology/immunology/genetics follow-up; schedule monitoring labs (CBC/retic after pyridoxine trial, iron overload monitoring, IVIG troughs if applicable) and developmental services.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 38-year-old anxious, vague speaker. Start with: “I’ve been exhausted for months and I get lightheaded when I stand.” Volunteer only: severe fatigue/weakness, dizziness/near-fainting on standing, always cold, weight gain despite no extra eating, more frequent headaches. If asked about pregnancy/postpartum: say after your last delivery your milk never really came in and your periods never returned. If asked endocrine/sexual/mood/GI: low libido, nausea/poor appetite, constipation, brain fog, thinning underarm/pubic hair, and occasional shaky/sweaty episodes when you haven’t eaten that improve with juice/snacks. Do NOT mention unless directly asked: heavy bleeding during delivery needing urgent care/extra fluids, low thirst with feeling dried out, low home blood pressures, poor stress tolerance, dry skin, easy bruising. Be resistant/embarrassed about vaginal dryness/pain with sex, inability to orgasm, and feeling down/tearful—minimize at first (“probably just stress/new mom”). Misconceptions: worry headaches mean a cyst/brain vessel problem; think it’s postpartum depression; believe low BP is “good”; think generic hormones are weaker and you sometimes stretch meds; worry about cost and missing work. confounders: Recent postpartum period with fatigue and mood changes initially attributed to postpartum depression

correct_diagnosis.primary: Sheehan syndrome (postpartum hypopituitarism with secondary adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism)
acceptable_alternatives: Hypopituitarism due to Rathke cleft cyst
Hypopituitarism due to pituitary epidermoid/dermoid cyst
required_evidence: Biochemical evidence of central adrenal insufficiency: low 8 AM serum cortisol with low or inappropriately normal plasma ACTH
Biochemical evidence of central hypothyroidism: low free T4 with low or inappropriately normal TSH
Biochemical evidence of hypogonadotropic hypogonadism: low estradiol with low or inappropriately normal LH/FSH
History compatible with postpartum pituitary infarction (e.g., severe postpartum hemorrhage/hypotension) and subsequent failure to lactate and/or amenorrhea (if history available)
Exclude primary thyroid/adrenal failure patterns (i.e., not high TSH with low FT4; not high ACTH with low cortisol)

expected_lab_results.Morning serum cortisol (8 AM): Low (suggesting adrenal insufficiency; typically <3–5 mcg/dL strongly suggestive)
expected_lab_results.Plasma ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal (consistent with secondary/central adrenal insufficiency)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal (not appropriately elevated for low FT4)
expected_lab_results.Prolactin: Low or inappropriately normal (often low in Sheehan due to lactotroph loss)

medications: Hydrocortisone 15 mg PO every morning and 5 mg PO mid-afternoon (or equivalent physiologic glucocorticoid replacement)
Levothyroxine 50 mcg PO daily (start after glucocorticoid replacement; titrate to free T4 in upper half of reference range)

Testosterone: hold/avoid unless clearly indicated and prescribed for a patient with established indication; if hypogonadism in a woman, use estrogen/progestin replacement instead of testosterone (specialist-guided)
non_pharmacological: Adrenal insufficiency education: sick-day rules (double/triple dose during febrile illness), do not stop steroids abruptly
Provide/arrange medical alert identification and emergency steroid plan; prescribe/teach use of IM hydrocortisone for vomiting/unable to take PO
Arrange pituitary MRI with contrast to evaluate pituitary morphology and exclude cystic lesions (Rathke cleft cyst, epidermoid/dermoid) if not already done
Assess for additional pituitary deficits (e.g., GH deficiency) and manage per endocrinology
Endocrinology referral for comprehensive long-term management and hormone replacement optimization
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1–2 weeks to review symptoms and free T4 response after starting therapy; earlier (24–72 hours) if symptomatic adrenal insufficiency or hypotension; ongoing endocrinology follow-up every 6–12 weeks during titration

must_include: Condition is hypopituitarism causing low pituitary hormones, leading to low cortisol and low thyroid hormone (and often low sex hormones)
Why Sheehan syndrome is suspected (postpartum pituitary injury context) and that other pituitary lesions may need imaging to exclude
Urgency: low cortisol can be life-threatening if untreated
Treat cortisol deficiency first with hydrocortisone before starting levothyroxine to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis
How to take hydrocortisone (timing, adherence) and levothyroxine (empty stomach, separate from iron/calcium)
Expected benefits and that doses will be adjusted based on symptoms and labs (free T4 rather than TSH in central hypothyroidism)
Review current testosterone use: confirm indication, dosing, and risks; avoid inappropriate androgen therapy in women and discuss alternative sex-hormone replacement if needed
Discuss warning signs requiring urgent care (severe weakness, vomiting, fainting, confusion, hypotension, fever unable to keep fluids down)
Confirm penicillin allergy details and document (urticarial rash) even though planned meds are non-penicillin
Sick-day rules and carrying steroid card/medical alert identification
When and how to use emergency injectable hydrocortisone and to seek emergency care after use
Importance of follow-up, repeat labs, and imaging to guide long-term management

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit postpartum history and symptoms suggestive of hypopituitarism (e.g., postpartum hemorrhage, failure to lactate, amenorrhea, fatigue, hypotension), current meds including testosterone, and red flags for adrenal crisis.

Order labs to confirm central adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism and assess other pituitary axes and electrolytes/glucose (e.g., 8am cortisol, ACTH, free T4, TSH, prolactin, LH/FSH, estradiol/testosterone as appropriate, IGF-1, CMP/Na/K/glucose, CBC; pregnancy test if relevant).
Interpret results consistent with hypopituitarism (low cortisol with low/inappropriately normal ACTH; low free T4 with low/inappropriately normal TSH; other pituitary hormone deficits).
Diagnose suspected Sheehan syndrome (postpartum pituitary infarction) while noting need to exclude other pituitary lesions with imaging.
Perform required allergy check before prescribing; confirm penicillin allergy details (urticarial rash) and document, even though planned meds are non-penicillin.
Check for potential interactions (non-critical) including iron/calcium/PPIs affecting levothyroxine absorption and other meds affecting steroid metabolism.

Initiate glucocorticoid replacement first (e.g., hydrocortisone) to treat cortisol deficiency and prevent adrenal crisis; then start levothyroxine after steroids are initiated; consider additional hormone replacement based on deficits and patient-specific factors.

Explain diagnosis, urgency, treatment sequencing (steroids before thyroid hormone), medication administration instructions, sick-day rules, emergency hydrocortisone use, and review testosterone use/risks and alternatives.
Arrange close follow-up for symptom review and lab titration (free T4-guided dosing in central hypothyroidism), endocrine referral, and pituitary MRI to exclude other lesions.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 14-year-old girl. You speak vaguely and try to minimize problems, especially if asked about missing school/work or costs. Start by saying you keep getting fevers for months with chills and exhaustion, plus a painful purple-red rash on hands/feet worse in cold, and morning joint aching/stiffness (knees/ankles/wrists). Also mention on-and-off face/eye swelling and unplanned weight loss.

Only share extra details if directly asked: tender lumps under skin, muscle aches/weakness, mouth sores during flares, fingers turning white/blue in cold then throbbing, bloating/loose stools, more frequent colds, and shortness of breath on stairs during flare weeks.

Do not reveal unless specifically asked about skin changes, urine, eyes, or night symptoms: tighter finger skin, fingertip cracks/slow healing, night sweats, thinning of arms/legs, dry gritty eyes, foamy urine.

Resist discussing alcohol, groin soreness/avoidance, and skipping meals unless asked privately and nonjudgmentally.

Express worries it might be lupus or scleroderma/Raynaud’s and fear your bones are “wearing away.” If the clinician shows empathy, you soften slightly but remain cautious about costs, insurance, and missing store shifts; assume only OTC vitamins/NSAIDs are affordable.

medications: prednisone 10 mg PO daily
naproxen 250 mg PO twice daily with food
ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO daily
vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) 1000 IU PO daily
hydrocortisone 1% topical cream apply thin layer twice daily as needed for rash
confounders: recurrent febrile episodes with elevated inflammatory markers mimicking infection despite negative cultures
chilblain-like acral rash and lipodystrophy overlapping with juvenile dermatomyositis or lupus features
chronic corticosteroid use masking fever and altering WBC count
microcytic anemia potentially attributed to nutritional deficiency rather than chronic inflammation

correct_diagnosis.primary: Proteasome-associated autoinflammatory syndrome (PRAAS), most consistent with CANDLE syndrome (chronic atypical neutrophilic dermatosis with lipodystrophy and elevated temperature)
acceptable_alternatives: Proteasome-associated autoinflammatory syndrome (PRAAS) due to PSMB8/other proteasome gene variant
CANDLE/PRAAS spectrum disorder
required_evidence: Chronic systemic inflammation with persistently elevated acute-phase reactants (CRP/ESR) and hyperferritinemia
CBC pattern compatible with chronic inflammation (anemia of inflammation and/or leukocytosis, thrombocytosis)

Clinical course suggestive of interferon-mediated autoinflammation (recurrent fevers and inflammatory skin/soft tissue manifestations and/or progressive lipodystrophy) with exclusion of infectious causes

Differential consideration documented and less consistent: MORA (PROM1) typically shows progressive osteolysis/arthropathy without marked systemic inflammatory labs; scleroderma/chilblain lupus typically have different serologic/clinical patterns and do not usually cause persistent marked hyperferritinemia
Plan for confirmatory testing (outside provided labs): type I interferon signature and/or genetic testing for proteasome pathway genes (e.g., PSMB8, PSMB9, PSMB4, PSMA3, POMP)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Mild to moderate leukocytosis (often neutrophil-predominant) during flares; can be persistently elevated
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normal or low-normal (normocytic to mildly microcytic anemia of inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Elevated (hyperferritinemia; can be markedly high in active disease)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - AST: Normal to mildly elevated (inflammation/medication-related; monitor for hepatotoxicity with immunomodulators)

medications: Baricitinib 2 mg PO once daily (titrate to 4 mg PO once daily if inadequate response and tolerated; dose-adjust for renal impairment)
Prednisone 0.5 mg/kg/day PO daily for acute flare control, then taper to the lowest effective dose as JAK inhibitor takes effect
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain (max 3,000 mg/day; lower max if liver disease or heavy alcohol use)
non_pharmacological: Avoid NSAIDs due to history of ibuprofen-associated facial swelling; use non-NSAID analgesia
Screen for infection risk and update immunizations prior to JAK inhibitor when feasible (influenza, pneumococcal, COVID-19; avoid live vaccines while on JAK inhibitor/high-dose steroids)
Arrange referral/coordination with rheumatology/immunology for interferonopathy management and genetic confirmation
Monitor growth/nutrition and counsel on bone health if prolonged steroids anticipated (calcium/vitamin D, weight-bearing activity as appropriate)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2–4 weeks after initiating therapy (earlier in 1 week if severe symptoms), then every 4–8 weeks with labs during dose titration

must_include: This is an autoinflammatory condition driven by an overactive innate immune pathway (often type I interferon signaling) rather than an infection
Reasoning based on persistent systemic inflammation on labs (high CRP/ESR/ferritin) and chronic inflammatory symptoms, with other listed differentials being less consistent
Need for confirmatory testing (genetic testing and/or interferon signature) and specialist involvement
Goal is to suppress interferon-driven inflammation and reduce steroid exposure
JAK inhibitor (e.g., baricitinib) is targeted therapy; steroids are for short-term control with a taper plan
Monitoring plan: CBC and liver enzymes/inflammatory markers at follow-up; watch for infection and thrombosis risk with JAK inhibitors
Infection risk counseling: seek care for fever, new cough, shortness of breath, painful skin lesions, or signs of shingles
Medication safety with allergies: avoid ibuprofen/NSAIDs; document beta-lactam allergy history (amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash) and discuss alternatives if antibiotics needed
Discuss potential adverse effects: steroids (weight gain, mood changes, hyperglycemia, bone loss) and JAK inhibitor (infection, cytopenias, LFT changes, thrombosis)
Avoid live vaccines while immunosuppressed; coordinate vaccine timing with clinicians
Hand hygiene and exposure precautions to reduce infections
Bone health and nutrition support if steroids are used (calcium/vitamin D, activity as tolerated)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for proteasome-associated autoinflammatory syndrome (PRAAS): chronic/recurrent fevers, rash/skin lesions, lipodystrophy, arthralgia/myalgia, fatigue, triggers, infection exposures, family history/consanguinity, prior workup, and current meds; screen for red flags requiring urgent care.

Order baseline inflammatory and safety labs to support diagnosis and prepare for immunosuppression: CBC with differential, CMP/LFTs, ESR/CRP, ferritin; add CK/LDH if myositis suspected; consider fasting lipids and glucose/A1c as baseline before steroids/JAK inhibitor.
Interpret results for persistent systemic inflammation (elevated CRP/ESR/ferritin) and assess cytopenias/LFT abnormalities that influence therapy choice and monitoring frequency.

State working diagnosis (suspected PRAAS/type I interferonopathy) with rationale and explicitly note need for confirmatory testing (genetic testing and/or interferon signature) and specialist involvement (rheumatology/immunology).

Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm and document allergy details (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash; NSAID/ibuprofen avoidance) and ensure proposed meds are safe given allergy history.

Noncritical safety check before prescribing: review current medications/supplements for interactions with steroids and JAK inhibitor (e.g., strong OAT3 inhibitors for baricitinib, other immunosuppressants, anticoagulants/antiplatelets as relevant).

Initiate appropriate therapy plan: short-term steroid bridge with taper plan and targeted therapy discussion/plan for JAK inhibitor (e.g., baricitinib) per specialist guidance; include monitoring and risk counseling.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (autoinflammatory, not infection; interferon-driven), explain treatment goals (reduce inflammation and steroid exposure), address concerns (infection signs, shingles, adverse effects, allergy-safe antibiotics/NSAID avoidance), and lifestyle/vaccine guidance (avoid live vaccines while immunosuppressed, hand hygiene, bone health support).
Arrange timely follow-up to reassess symptoms, review labs (CBC/LFTs/inflammatory markers), monitor for infection/thrombosis, and coordinate genetic testing/interferon signature and specialist care.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

Initiate treatment to raise/maintain glucose (IV dextrose bolus and continuous infusion; adjust to keep euglycemia), stop catabolism, treat vomiting with appropriate non-sorbitol formulation, and specify frequent glucose checks and repeat labs; consider bicarbonate only for severe acidemia/hemodynamic instability with reassessment via repeat blood gas.

Order monitoring labs: repeat glucose checks (q15–60 min initially then spaced), repeat VBG/ABG, lactate, electrolytes/anion gap, bicarbonate; monitor for rebound hypoglycemia and resolution of acidosis.
Verify improving glucose and closing anion gap/lactate; adjust dextrose infusion and disposition (floor vs ICU) accordingly.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, 34, a night-shift warehouse forklift operator with a high-school education. You speak vaguely and try to keep the visit short because you fear costs and missing work. Start by saying: “I keep getting sick over and over, and this cough just won’t go away.” Volunteer only: frequent “chest infections” needing antibiotics, cough for weeks, feeling wiped out, unintentional weight loss, and on/off fevers mostly at night. correct_diagnosis.primary: T-B+ severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) due to CD3 delta/epsilon/zeta (CD3D/CD3E/CD247) deficiency
acceptable_alternatives: Severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID), T-cell receptor/CD3 complex defect (T-B+ phenotype)
Combined immunodeficiency with profound T-cell lymphopenia (T-B+ phenotype)
required_evidence: Marked lymphopenia with very low absolute lymphocyte count (ALC), especially in an infant/young child with recurrent/severe infections
Very low CD4+ and CD8+ T-cell counts consistent with profound T-cell deficiency
Relative preservation of non–T-cell lineages suggested by normal/near-normal ANC and platelets/hemoglobin (unless infection-related changes)

Clinical context consistent with immunodeficiency rather than isolated pulmonary neuroendocrine tumor/carcinoid syndrome or amoebiasis (no carcinoid flushing/diarrhea pattern; no invasive dysentery/liver abscess features)

Need for confirmatory immunology/genetics: absent/markedly reduced T-cell function (e.g., poor mitogen response) and pathogenic variant in CD3D/CD3E/CD247 (not required for initial stabilization but required for definitive diagnosis)

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 42-year-old anxious but clear-speaking accounting manager. Start by saying: “I keep losing my balance and I’m getting more clumsy when I walk.” Early in the visit, volunteer that you stumble more when turning or in the dark, your hands shake with fine tasks (buttons/utensils), and your feet sometimes feel numb/asleep. If asked, add slurred/tipsy-sounding speech, occasional coughing/choking with water, eyes that “jump” when shifting gaze, dizziness on standing, a few recent falls, messier/larger handwriting, and tingling spreading up to the ankles. Do not volunteer urinary urgency/accidents, constipation, fatigue, night leg cramps, or holding onto furniture unless specifically asked; if asked directly, admit them. Be resistant/embarrassed about urine accidents and avoiding going out; disclose only after rapport or reassurance. Express misconceptions: you suspect inner ear issues, low blood sugar (shaky/sweaty), or even stroke; you’ve blamed aging/out-of-shape. Mention cost worries if treatment/testing comes up (riluzole, liver labs, PT, genetic testing). If the clinician shows empathy, you relax slightly, thank them, and share more detail.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: you returned from a mosquito-heavy trip 5 days ago and since then you’ve had an itchy red rash (started on chest, spread to arms/legs), low-grade fever/chills, headache, fatigue, sore joints (wrists/ankles), and red irritated eyes. Volunteer these early.
If asked, add: fever started first, rash began ~1 day later; morning puffiness/stiff hands/feet; mild nausea/low appetite; light sensitivity/gritty eyes; small tender neck lumps; multiple mosquito bites, inconsistent repellent; no bleeding.
Withhold unless directly asked about pregnancy/sex: you’re trying to get pregnant, period ~1 week late; unprotected sex during/after trip; partner had 1 day of mild fever. If asked about new partners, hesitate and say you’re embarrassed, then disclose unprotected sex with a new partner while traveling.
Express misconceptions: worry your joints are “wearing away,” and swelling might mean kidney problems.
If clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and share sensitive details. Ask for the quickest, lowest-cost plan; worry about missing work and employer reactions.

medications: Prenatal multivitamin 1 tablet PO daily
Loratadine 10 mg PO daily PRN allergic rhinitis
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours PRN fever/pain (max 3 g/day)
confounders: Recent travel to a mosquito-endemic region with Aedes exposure
Dengue serology cross-reactivity (equivocal dengue IgM) complicating interpretation of flavivirus testing
Early pregnancy with similar nonspecific symptoms (fatigue, mild nausea) that may mask viral prodrome
Recent use of acetaminophen prior to presentation potentially blunting fever
Partner recently returned from same region with mild symptoms (possible sexual transmission route)

differentials: multicentric carpo-tarsal osteolysis with or without nephropathy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Zika virus infectious disease
acceptable_alternatives: Acute viral syndrome (arboviral illness, suspected Zika)
Zika virus infection (probable, pending confirmatory testing)
required_evidence: Compatible epidemiologic exposure (recent travel to or residence in Zika-affected area OR unprotected sex with potentially exposed partner OR mosquito exposure in endemic region) within prior 2–14 days
Acute onset of mild febrile illness or afebrile viral syndrome with maculopapular rash and/or arthralgia and/or nonpurulent conjunctivitis
No focal bacterial source; clinical course consistent with self-limited viral illness
CBC without marked leukocytosis; platelets not severely low
CMP without significant hepatic injury or renal dysfunction suggesting alternative diagnosis
Consider NAAT/RT-PCR on serum/urine early in illness and Zika IgM later (as available) to support diagnosis

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal or mildly low (mild leukopenia possible); no significant neutrophilic leukocytosis
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal or mildly decreased; not severely thrombocytopenic

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever or pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower max if liver disease or heavy alcohol use)
non_pharmacological: Supportive care: oral hydration, rest
Avoid NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen, naproxen) and aspirin until dengue is reasonably excluded due to bleeding risk; also avoid ibuprofen specifically due to allergy
Mosquito bite prevention for at least 3 weeks after symptom onset to reduce onward transmission (repellent, long sleeves, stay indoors with screens/AC)
Sexual transmission prevention: use condoms/abstain; if pregnant partner or pregnancy possible, emphasize strict condom use and follow local public health guidance
If pregnancy is possible or patient is pregnant: urgent obstetric evaluation and Zika-specific counseling/testing per guidelines
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 24–48 hours if worsening; otherwise follow up with primary care within 3–7 days or sooner for pregnancy-related concerns or neurologic symptoms

must_include: Likely Zika based on exposure risk and symptom pattern; typically self-limited
Discuss need/limitations of confirmatory testing (timing of PCR vs IgM; cross-reactivity with other flaviviruses)
Explain key alternatives to consider (especially dengue/chikungunya) and why management is cautious about NSAIDs/aspirin initially
Supportive care only; no specific antiviral routinely recommended
Use acetaminophen for fever/pain; avoid ibuprofen due to allergy and avoid NSAIDs/aspirin until dengue is excluded
Hydration and rest; expected recovery over days to about a week
Pregnancy/fetal risk counseling: Zika can cause congenital infection; assess pregnancy status and partner pregnancy status; offer testing/referral as appropriate

Red flags requiring urgent care: severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, bleeding, severe headache/neck stiffness, confusion, shortness of breath, weakness/numbness (possible neurologic complication such as Guillain-Barré)
Discuss when to return if symptoms worsen or new symptoms develop
Mosquito avoidance to prevent spread to others for at least 3 weeks after symptom onset
Sexual transmission precautions (condoms/abstinence) and duration per public health guidance
Avoid donating blood/semen for the recommended deferral period per local policy

Believes muscle weakness means he should avoid all exercise because it will 'wear out' his nerves faster.
Assumes numbness and burning pain mean his spine is 'slipping' and that he needs surgery to fix the problem.
Thinks braces and orthotics will make his ankles weaker and cause permanent dependence.
Believes genetic conditions are always inherited from the mother and is angry/defensive when family history is discussed.
Thinks vitamins or 'nerve detox' supplements can reverse nerve damage and is skeptical of rehab/physical therapy.
