patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male forklift operator. You speak vaguely and avoid details unless directly asked. Start with: “I keep tripping over my feet, and my ankles feel weak.” If the clinician asks for more, add: feet feel “floppy,” don’t lift well, you tire quickly on uneven ground, and shoes wear out on the outside edge. Only if specifically asked about feet shape, injuries, sensation, hands, pain at night, stairs/running, or timeline, reveal: high arches/claw toes for years, frequent ankle sprains, thinning calves, toe numbness/tingling, weaker hands, burning/aching at night, slower stairs, long-standing but worsening over 2 years.

Do not volunteer: poor heat/pebble sensation, unnoticed blisters/sores, balance worse in dark/shower, knee-lifting “high step,” shoe fit/pressure spots—share only if asked about falls, sensation, wounds, balance, or gait.

Resist discussing embarrassment, avoiding outings, holding rails, intimacy issues unless asked gently.

Express misconceptions: think it’s a pinched back nerve, worry about diabetes or ALS, or overtraining.

If the clinician is empathetic, you soften slightly but still worry about being fired, costs of PT/braces, disability ruining future work/coverage, and genetic testing affecting insurance.

medications: gabapentin 300 mg PO three times daily
ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain
sertraline 50 mg PO daily
comorbidities: recurrent ankle sprains due to distal weakness
confounders: borderline low vitamin B12 with mildly elevated methylmalonic acid (can mimic/worsen neuropathy)
history of heavy alcohol use in early 20s (possible toxic neuropathy contribution)
frequent NSAID use for musculoskeletal pain (may mask inflammatory symptoms and complicate pain assessment)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy (HMSN)
CMT1 (demyelinating Charcot-Marie-Tooth) or CMT2 (axonal Charcot-Marie-Tooth)
required_evidence: Chronic, slowly progressive distal symmetric weakness and atrophy (especially feet/lower legs) with sensory loss
Foot deformities (pes cavus and/or hammertoes) and frequent ankle sprains/foot drop
Reduced/absent ankle reflexes
Length-dependent peripheral neuropathy pattern (distal > proximal) with gait disturbance/steppage gait
Family history of similar neuropathy and/or onset in childhood/adolescence or early adulthood
Exclusion of common acquired neuropathy mimics using available labs (diabetes, B12 deficiency, thyroid disease, inflammatory myopathy)

expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Normal or non-diagnostic for diabetic neuropathy (typically <5.7%); if mildly elevated, insufficient to explain longstanding hereditary pattern
expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal or mildly elevated (not in myositis range; neuropathy pattern predominates)

medications: Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 1200 mg/day OTC; avoid if CKD, PUD, anticoagulation)
non_pharmacological: Referral to neuromuscular neurology for confirmation/phenotyping and consideration of genetic testing and counseling
Physical therapy for strengthening, stretching, balance training, and fall-prevention program
Occupational therapy for hand weakness, adaptive devices, and energy conservation strategies
Ankle-foot orthoses (AFO) for foot drop; custom orthotics for pes cavus; supportive footwear
Podiatry/orthopedics evaluation for progressive deformity, recurrent ulcers, or surgical consideration (e.g., tendon transfer/osteotomy) when indicated
Screen for and mitigate fall risks at home; gait aid if needed

Avoid known neurotoxic medications when possible (e.g., vincristine, cisplatin, paclitaxel, high-dose pyridoxine, nitrofurantoin, metronidazole long-term, amiodarone, linezolid) and review all meds/supplements
Patient education on daily foot inspection and skin care; prompt attention to pressure points/blisters
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptoms, function, and safety in 4-8 weeks after initiating PT/orthotics and pain plan; earlier (1-2 weeks) if rapid worsening, new proximal weakness, severe pain, or frequent falls

must_include: CMT is a hereditary (genetic) peripheral neuropathy affecting motor and sensory nerves
Typical course is slowly progressive over years; symptoms often start earlier in life and can run in families
Current labs do not support common acquired causes (diabetes, B12 deficiency, thyroid disease, inflammatory process) as the primary driver
Confirmatory evaluation typically involves neuromuscular exam and may include nerve conduction studies and genetic testing
No cure, but function and quality of life can improve with PT/OT, braces/orthotics, and foot care
Pain management focuses on safe, symptom-based options; escalation to neuropathic pain agents can be considered if pain is neuropathic and persistent
Importance of specialist follow-up for tailored management and genetic counseling
Discuss prognosis realistically: slow progression, variable severity, many people remain ambulatory with supports
Discuss inheritance and implications for family members; offer genetic counseling
Red flags that warrant urgent reassessment (rapid progression, asymmetric deficits, new bowel/bladder symptoms, severe new pain, systemic symptoms)
Daily foot inspection, protective footwear, and prompt care for sores to prevent complications
Fall-prevention strategies and home safety modifications
Avoidance of neurotoxic medications and excessive alcohol; review supplements (avoid high-dose vitamin B6)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history to support hereditary peripheral neuropathy vs acquired causes; characterize onset, progression, distribution, functional impact, family history, and red flags.
Medication/substance/supplement review (including alcohol and vitamin B6), prior neurotoxic exposures, and prior evaluations; establish baseline comorbidities relevant to contraindications.
Order screening labs to exclude common acquired neuropathy causes (e.g., A1c/fasting glucose, B12 with MMA if needed, TSH, CMP, CBC +/- ESR/CRP as appropriate) consistent with the context statement.
Confirm that labs do not support diabetes/B12 deficiency/thyroid disease/inflammatory process as primary driver; document and integrate into diagnostic reasoning.

Make a working diagnosis of Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary peripheral neuropathy) based on clinical pattern, family history, and exclusion of common acquired causes; recommend confirmatory evaluation (neuromuscular exam, NCS/EMG, genetic testing).
Explain diagnosis in plain language, expected course, and confirmatory next steps; address patient concerns, inheritance, prognosis, and red flags.
Explain treatment and lifestyle: PT/OT, braces/orthotics, foot care, fall prevention, avoidance of neurotoxins/excess alcohol, and supplement safety (avoid high-dose B6).
Required critical safety check before any prescribing for pain or other symptoms.
Required critical safety check before any prescribing; ensure proposed agents are appropriate given comorbidities/pregnancy risk/renal-hepatic status and CMT considerations.
Non-critical but expected safety check before prescribing to reduce adverse events and polypharmacy risk.
If pain is present and persistent, provide symptom-based pain management; consider neuropathic pain agents only when neuropathic features are present and after safety checks.
Arrange neuromuscular specialist referral, PT/OT, orthotics/podiatry as needed, and genetic counseling; set follow-up to reassess function, pain control, and safety.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute gastroenteritis with dehydration (likely viral)
acceptable_alternatives: Foodborne gastroenteritis (toxin-mediated)
Mild bacterial gastroenteritis without dysentery
Acute diarrhea due to medication intolerance (if exposure history supports)
required_evidence: Acute onset nausea/vomiting and/or diarrhea (typically <7 days)
Clinical dehydration (e.g., thirst, dry mucous membranes, orthostatic symptoms, decreased urine output)
No red-flag features suggesting invasive infection (e.g., no bloody stools, severe abdominal pain, high persistent fever) or surgical abdomen
Basic labs consistent with dehydration (e.g., elevated BUN and/or electrolyte abnormalities)
Exposure history supportive (sick contacts, recent travel, questionable food) OR absence of alternative explanation

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (stress leukocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Normal to mildly elevated due to hemoconcentration
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Normal to mildly low or mildly high depending on free-water intake vs losses
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Often low if significant diarrhea/vomiting; can be normal early
expected_lab_results.Chloride: May be low with vomiting; may be normal/high with diarrhea
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low (non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis) if diarrhea predominant; may be high (metabolic alkalosis) if vomiting predominant

medications: Oral rehydration solution 500-1000 mL PO over 1-2 hours then 200 mL PO after each loose stool
Ondansetron ODT 4 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for nausea/vomiting (avoid if significant QT-prolongation risk)
Loperamide 4 mg PO once then 2 mg PO after each loose stool (max 8 mg/day OTC) ONLY if no fever or blood in stool
non_pharmacological: Assess severity of dehydration; consider IV fluids (0.9% saline bolus) if unable to tolerate PO, orthostasis, or significant electrolyte derangements
Diet as tolerated (small frequent sips/foods); avoid alcohol and high-sugar drinks; continue breastfeeding if applicable
Hand hygiene and infection control; avoid preparing food for others until symptoms resolve
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24-48 hours (sooner same-day/ED if worsening, unable to keep fluids down, confusion, syncope, severe abdominal pain, bloody stools, or persistent high fever)

must_include: Likely self-limited stomach/intestinal infection causing fluid loss and dehydration
What features would suggest a more serious cause (blood in stool, severe pain, persistent high fever, signs of severe dehydration)
Primary treatment is rehydration (how to use oral rehydration solution and goals for urine output)
How and when to use antiemetic/antidiarrheal and when to avoid loperamide (fever/bloody stool)
When IV fluids or urgent evaluation is needed
Expected course (improvement over 1-3 days for viral illness; sometimes up to a week)
Return precautions and escalation plan
Medication side effects (ondansetron constipation/headache; loperamide constipation, avoid in dysentery)
Hydration strategy and diet as tolerated
Infection prevention (handwashing, avoid sharing utensils, stay home from work/school while symptomatic)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history to assess dehydration severity, infectious features, and red flags (blood, severe pain, persistent high fever), plus exposures and baseline risks.
Order only if indicated at L1 (e.g., concerning dehydration, prolonged symptoms, significant comorbidity) to assess electrolytes/renal function and rule out serious infection when red flags present.
Use results to confirm hydration status/complications (AKI, electrolyte derangements) and adjust disposition (home vs urgent/ED).
Provide working diagnosis: likely acute gastroenteritis with dehydration; state uncertainty and differential triggers for escalation.
Critical safety check before any medication prescribing.
Optional safety check before prescribing to identify clinically meaningful interactions (e.g., QT-prolonging meds with ondansetron).
Offer symptomatic therapy when appropriate (e.g., ondansetron; cautious loperamide use), ensuring no red flags and safety checks completed.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis/treatment, hydration strategy, medication use/avoidance, expected course, infection prevention, and return precautions.
Arrange appropriate follow-up timeframe and contingency plan based on severity and risk factors.
Close encounter after confirming understanding and documenting escalation plan.

nl_assertions: Agent asks about duration and frequency of vomiting/diarrhea, ability to keep fluids down, and quantifies dehydration (thirst, dizziness/orthostasis, dry mouth, urine output/last void).

Agent screens for red flags: blood or mucus in stool, severe or localized abdominal pain, persistent high fever, confusion/lethargy, syncope, inability to tolerate oral intake, and signs of severe dehydration.
Agent asks about exposure risks (sick contacts, travel, foodborne exposures), recent antibiotics, and relevant comorbidities (pregnancy, immunocompromise, kidney disease, cardiac disease).

Agent provides a working diagnosis consistent with likely self-limited stomach/intestinal infection causing fluid loss and dehydration, while explicitly naming features that would suggest a more serious cause (blood in stool, severe pain, persistent high fever, severe dehydration).
Agent explains primary treatment is rehydration and gives actionable oral rehydration guidance (small frequent sips, ORS use) and a goal for urine output/voiding frequency as a marker of improvement.

Agent discusses antiemetic/antidiarrheal use, including when to avoid loperamide (fever or bloody stool/suspected dysentery) and provides expected side effects (ondansetron constipation/headache; loperamide constipation).

Agent states when IV fluids or urgent evaluation is needed (worsening dehydration, inability to keep fluids down, severe weakness/syncope, ongoing high fever, bloody stool, severe abdominal pain, confusion).
Agent provides expected course (often improves over 1–3 days if viral; sometimes up to a week) and gives clear return precautions and escalation plan.
Agent educates on hydration strategy and diet as tolerated and includes infection prevention (handwashing, avoid sharing utensils, stay home from work/school while symptomatic).
Before any prescribing, agent completes allergy_check and contraindication_check; failure to do so is a critical error.
If prescribing loperamide, agent confirms absence of fever/bloody stool and no suspicion for invasive bacterial diarrhea; if these are present, loperamide is avoided.

If prescribing ondansetron, agent considers QT-risk context (history of long QT, electrolyte abnormalities, concurrent QT-prolonging drugs) and adjusts plan accordingly; interaction check is performed when feasible but is non-critical.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation (NBIA) – Pantothenate kinase–associated neurodegeneration (PKAN) phenotype
acceptable_alternatives: Neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation (NBIA) – PLA2G6-associated neurodegeneration (PLAN)
Neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation (NBIA) – MPAN (C19orf12-associated neurodegeneration)
Wilson disease (hepatic/neurologic copper accumulation)
Juvenile-onset Huntington disease
Dopa-responsive dystonia
Atypical Parkinsonism with dystonia (e.g., PSP-C, CBD)
Hereditary spastic paraplegia with dystonia
required_evidence: Progressive extrapyramidal syndrome with dystonia and/or parkinsonism and gait impairment
Supportive history of neurodegenerative progression (childhood/adolescent/young adult onset depending on subtype) with functional decline
Brain MRI evidence of basal ganglia iron deposition (e.g., T2*/SWI hypointensity in globus pallidus/substantia nigra); if PKAN suspected, 'eye-of-the-tiger' sign in globus pallidus
Exclusion of key mimics when clinically indicated (e.g., Wilson disease with ceruloplasmin/serum copper/24h urine copper; medication-induced dystonia/parkinsonism history)
Genetic confirmation when available (e.g., PANK2, PLA2G6, C19orf12 or NBIA panel)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no specific NBIA-associated anemia expected)

medications: Trihexyphenidyl 1 mg PO BID, titrate every 3-7 days as tolerated toward 2-5 mg PO TID for dystonia
Baclofen 5 mg PO TID, titrate every 3-7 days as tolerated (typical target 10-20 mg PO TID) for spasticity/dystonia
Carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 mg PO TID trial for parkinsonism (continue only if objective benefit)
Botulinum toxin injection (onabotulinumtoxinA) per affected muscles and standard dosing intervals (typically every 12 weeks) for focal dystonia/spasticity
Diazepam 2 mg PO q8-12h PRN severe dystonia/spasms (use lowest effective dose; avoid routine long-term use if possible)
non_pharmacological: Refer to movement-disorders neurology for NBIA subtype workup (MRI SWI/T2* and genetic testing) and advanced therapy consideration
Physical and occupational therapy for gait, stretching, contracture prevention, assistive device evaluation, fall-prevention plan
Speech-language pathology for dysarthria/dysphagia screening; swallow evaluation if choking/weight loss/aspiration risk
Consider deep brain stimulation of GPi for medically refractory, function-limiting dystonia (specialist evaluation)
Nutritional assessment; consider gastrostomy planning if progressive dysphagia/aspiration risk
Supportive care planning: psychosocial support, caregiver training, community resources, and advance care planning as disease progresses
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up in 4-6 weeks after initiating/titrating symptomatic therapy; sooner (1-2 weeks) if rapid decline, severe dystonia, dysphagia, or medication adverse effects

must_include: NBIA is a group of genetic neurodegenerative disorders characterized by abnormal iron accumulation in deep brain structures
Diagnosis is supported by characteristic MRI findings and confirmed with genetic testing when possible
Course is progressive; symptoms often include dystonia, parkinsonism, spasticity, speech/swallow issues, and functional decline
Important mimics (especially Wilson disease) may need targeted testing depending on age and presentation
No definitive cure; treatment focuses on symptom control and maintaining function/quality of life
Medication goals and key risks: anticholinergic side effects (confusion, urinary retention, constipation), baclofen sedation/weakness, benzodiazepine sedation/respiratory depression/falls
Role of botulinum toxin for focal symptoms and DBS for refractory generalized dystonia (specialist-led)
Need for careful titration and monitoring for benefit vs adverse effects
Discuss prognosis uncertainty by subtype and individual variability; set realistic expectations
Review safety planning: fall risk, choking/aspiration warning signs, when to seek urgent care
Genetic counseling implications for family members and future pregnancies
Confirm and document penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and explain it does not generally affect the listed symptomatic neurologic medications
Home safety modifications and assistive devices to reduce falls
Daily stretching/ROM program to prevent contractures
Swallow safety strategies if dysphagia present (texture modification, supervised meals) pending SLP guidance
Caregiver support resources and planning for progressive care needs

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms, progression, onset age, functional impact, family history/consanguinity, medication history, and red flags (dysphagia/aspiration, falls, respiratory issues).

Order targeted testing to evaluate mimics/secondary causes as appropriate (especially Wilson disease depending on age/presentation; liver tests; copper studies; other relevant metabolic/infectious tests as clinically indicated).
Review ordered lab results to support/triage differential and guide next diagnostic steps.
State NBIA as the working diagnosis when clinical picture and characteristic MRI are present; note need for genetic confirmation when possible and document differential/mimics addressed.
Explain diagnosis (NBIA definition, MRI/genetics, progressive course, common symptoms, key mimics like Wilson disease) and address patient/caregiver questions.
Before any prescribing, confirm and document allergy history (including penicillin urticarial rash) and relevance to planned symptomatic medications.
Before prescribing, review current meds/substances (including sedatives, anticholinergics) for interactions with proposed symptomatic therapies (e.g., baclofen, benzodiazepines, anticholinergics).
Initiate symptom-directed therapy with careful titration plan and monitoring (e.g., dystonia/spasticity management), only after required safety checks are completed.

Explain treatment approach (no cure; symptom control), medication goals/risks (anticholinergic effects; baclofen sedation/weakness; benzodiazepine sedation/respiratory depression/falls), and specialist options (botulinum toxin/DBS).

Lifestyle/safety counseling: fall prevention, home modifications, stretching/ROM, swallow safety strategies pending SLP, caregiver supports, genetic counseling implications, and urgent return precautions.
Arrange close follow-up to assess response/adverse effects, titrate therapy, coordinate neurology/genetics/rehab/SLP, and update care planning.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and safety-net instructions.

patient.chief_complaint: I get out of breath really easily, even doing simple things.

volunteer: I get out of breath walking up a short flight of stairs or carrying groceries
I’m more tired than usual and have less stamina than I used to
Sometimes my heart feels like it’s racing when I’m just walking around
if_asked: I sometimes feel lightheaded, especially if I stand up fast or walk uphill
I’ve had a couple moments where I thought I might faint, but I didn’t fully pass out
I get a tight, pressure-like feeling in my chest when I’m exerting myself that goes away when I rest
My ankles and lower legs have been swelling by the end of the day
I’ve noticed I need to prop myself up with extra pillows sometimes to feel more comfortable breathing at night
My lips or fingertips have looked a little bluish when I’m really winded, according to my partner
hidden: I’ve been avoiding activities without really thinking about it—like parking closer or taking elevators—because I don’t want to get winded
I feel more short of breath in hot weather or when I’m rushing
I’ve had a dry cough off and on, but it’s not severe
resistant: My sex drive is down because I get winded and tired easily, and it’s embarrassing to talk about
misleading: I wondered if it’s asthma because I get short of breath, but inhalers I tried from a friend didn’t really help
I thought it might be anxiety because sometimes the fast heartbeat makes me feel panicky
noise: I’ve had some mild heartburn after spicy foods
My seasonal allergies have been acting up with a stuffy nose

progressive_reveal.symptom: I sometimes feel lightheaded, especially if I stand up fast or walk uphill
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about dizziness or feeling faint with activity

progressive_reveal.symptom: I get a tight, pressure-like feeling in my chest when I’m exerting myself that goes away when I rest
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked specifically about chest discomfort or pain during exertion

progressive_reveal.symptom: My ankles and lower legs have been swelling by the end of the day
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about swelling, weight changes, or shoe tightness

progressive_reveal.symptom: My sex drive is down because I get winded and tired easily, and it’s embarrassing to talk about
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed only after reassurance and a direct question about how symptoms affect intimacy or daily life

general_concerns: I thought pulmonary hypertension is basically the same as regular high blood pressure and should improve if I just cut salt and lose weight.
Because my chest X-ray and basic labs were mostly normal, I’m worried the diagnosis might be a mistake and it’s just anxiety or being out of shape.
If I avoid exercise completely, I won’t strain my heart and the condition won’t get worse.
Oxygen is addictive, so I should only use it if I’m extremely short of breath.
If I feel better on medication, that probably means I’m cured and can stop treatment.
economic_concerns: I’m worried the newer pulmonary hypertension medications will be too expensive even with insurance, and I might have to ration them.
I’m not sure if frequent specialist visits and tests are really necessary, because missing work and copays add up.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 32-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I get out of breath really easily, even doing simple things.” Volunteer early: you get winded walking up a short flight of stairs or carrying groceries, you’re more tired with less stamina, and sometimes your heart races even with light activity. If asked about associated symptoms, disclose: occasional lightheadedness (especially standing fast or uphill) with near-fainting, exertional chest tightness/pressure that resolves with rest, ankle/lower-leg swelling by day’s end, needing extra pillows to breathe comfortably at night, and partner noticing bluish lips/fingertips when very winded. Only if asked further, mention you’ve been unconsciously avoiding exertion, heat/rushing makes it worse, and an intermittent dry cough. Do not mention decreased sex drive unless directly asked; then admit embarrassment. Express misconceptions: you wondered about asthma (borrowed inhaler didn’t help) or anxiety because palpitations feel panicky. If clinician shows empathy, respond appreciatively and become more open. Bring up cost/work concerns: fear PAH meds are expensive, rationing, and doubts about frequent specialist visits/tests due to copays and missed work.

medications: Tadalafil 40 mg PO once daily
Ambrisentan 10 mg PO once daily
Furosemide 20 mg PO once daily
Spironolactone 25 mg PO once daily
Warfarin 5 mg PO once daily (dose adjusted to INR goal 2.0-3.0)
Norethindrone 0.35 mg PO once daily (progestin-only contraception)
comorbidities: Right ventricular dysfunction secondary to pulmonary arterial hypertension
Chronic venous congestion with mild hepatic dysfunction
allergies: Sulfonamide antibiotics (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole): urticaria and facial swelling
confounders: Mild intermittent asthma with episodic wheeze and cough
History of stimulant appetite suppressant use (phentermine) 5 years ago
Generalized anxiety with panic episodes causing dyspnea and chest tightness
Current use of progestin-only contraception; pregnancy must still be excluded when symptoms worsen

correct_diagnosis.primary: Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (IPAH, WHO Group 1)
acceptable_alternatives: Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH), WHO Group 1 (unspecified etiology)
Heritable pulmonary arterial hypertension (if family history/genetic testing suggests)
required_evidence: Pre-capillary pulmonary hypertension on right-heart catheterization: mean pulmonary artery pressure > 20 mmHg with pulmonary capillary wedge pressure ≤ 15 mmHg and pulmonary vascular resistance > 2 Wood units
No significant parenchymal lung disease or chronic thromboembolic disease to explain PH (e.g., imaging/VQ/CTPA negative for CTEPH; PFTs not consistent with severe obstructive/restrictive disease)
Symptoms/signs consistent with PAH (e.g., progressive exertional dyspnea, fatigue, syncope/presyncope, chest discomfort; evidence of RV strain/heart failure)
Risk stratification evidence (e.g., BNP/NT-proBNP elevation and/or clinical signs of right-sided heart strain) supporting PAH severity assessment

expected_lab_results.BNP: Often elevated (reflecting right ventricular strain); may be normal in early/mild disease
expected_lab_results.NT-proBNP: Often elevated and correlates with risk; higher values suggest worse prognosis/right ventricular dysfunction
expected_lab_results.Troponin I (high-sensitivity): Usually normal; may be mildly elevated in advanced PAH with RV ischemia/strain (worse prognosis)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be normal; can be elevated if chronic hypoxemia (secondary erythrocytosis) or low if iron deficiency/anemia of chronic disease
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: Mirrors hemoglobin; may be elevated with chronic hypoxemia or low with anemia
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Typically normal; may be low in advanced right heart failure/congestion or due to medications if present
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often normal; may be low (hyponatremia) in advanced right heart failure/volume overload and is a poor prognostic sign

medications: Furosemide 20-40 mg PO daily (titrate to euvolemia if volume overloaded/right heart failure signs)
Ambrisentan 5 mg PO daily (increase to 10 mg PO daily as tolerated) + Tadalafil 40 mg PO daily (initial combination therapy for low-risk IPAH, if no contraindications)
If vasoreactivity testing is positive: Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily (or Nifedipine ER 30 mg PO daily) titrate as tolerated (high-dose CCB therapy in responders only)
Supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥ 90% (if hypoxemic)
non_pharmacological: Confirm risk stratification and guideline-directed evaluation (functional class, 6-minute walk distance, echocardiography, right-heart catheterization data review)
Supervised exercise/rehabilitation program as tolerated; avoid heavy isometric exertion
Vaccinations: annual influenza and age-/risk-appropriate pneumococcal vaccination
Pregnancy avoidance and contraception counseling (PAH carries high maternal mortality; endothelin receptor antagonists are teratogenic)
Low-sodium diet and daily weights if fluid retention; education on recognizing right heart failure symptoms
Referral/management in a pulmonary hypertension specialty center
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-12 weeks after therapy initiation (earlier if symptomatic worsening), then every 3-6 months with repeat risk assessment (symptoms, BNP/NT-proBNP, functional capacity, echocardiography as indicated)

must_include: Definition: high blood pressure in the lung arteries due to narrowing/stiffening, causing strain on the right side of the heart
Idiopathic means no secondary cause identified after evaluation
Why it matters: can progress to right heart failure; prognosis improves with early, targeted therapy
Need for ongoing monitoring and specialty follow-up
Goals: improve symptoms/exercise capacity, slow progression, reduce hospitalization and mortality risk
Targeted PAH therapy options and rationale (endothelin pathway + nitric oxide pathway; escalation if not meeting low-risk targets)
Diuretics are for fluid overload symptoms and do not treat the underlying vessel disease
Key adverse effects and what to do (e.g., hypotension, edema, headache; seek urgent care for syncope, chest pain, severe dyspnea)
Discuss pregnancy risk and need for reliable contraception; teratogenicity risk with endothelin receptor antagonists
Review medication affordability/access and adherence plan
When to seek urgent care: fainting, worsening shortness of breath at rest, chest pain, new leg swelling, hemoptysis
Activity guidance: paced aerobic activity; avoid heavy lifting/straining; stop if dizzy or severely short of breath
Avoid high altitude and situations that worsen hypoxemia if applicable; use prescribed oxygen
Low-sodium diet and daily weights if fluid retention; limit alcohol; avoid smoking
Keep vaccinations up to date and avoid respiratory infections when possible

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key symptoms, functional class, red flags, pregnancy/contraception status, comorbidities, and current meds to frame PAH severity and immediate safety.

Order baseline monitoring labs commonly used in PAH care and medication safety (e.g., CMP/LFTs, renal function/electrolytes, CBC; pregnancy test if of childbearing potential; BNP/NT-proBNP if available).
Review results to support risk assessment and ensure safe prescribing/ordering (renal function, LFTs, electrolytes, pregnancy status where relevant).
Critical safety check before prescribing any PAH-targeted therapy or supportive meds.
Critical safety check before prescribing; identify contraindicated combinations (e.g., PDE-5 inhibitor with nitrates/riociguat; strong CYP interactions for certain agents).
Critical safety check before prescribing; ensure no duplicative or hazardous vasodilator regimens.
Critical safety check before ordering tests/therapies (e.g., pregnancy-related constraints, severe hypotension, significant hepatic impairment for certain therapies).
Critical safety check before prescribing PAH therapies (e.g., pregnancy for ERAs; concomitant riociguat + PDE-5 inhibitor; severe hepatic impairment for select agents).
State idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension as the working diagnosis (assuming evaluation has excluded secondary causes per task context) and communicate clinical implications.

Initiate guideline-consistent initial PAH-targeted therapy appropriate for L0 difficulty (typically oral combination therapy in low/intermediate risk, or monotherapy if clearly low risk/contraindications), plus supportive therapy if indicated (e.g., diuretic for volume overload).
Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment goals/options/side effects, address pregnancy/contraception and urgent symptoms, and lifestyle guidance.

Arrange specialty follow-up and monitoring (symptoms/functional class, vitals, labs, pregnancy testing if ERA used, and response-to-therapy reassessment with escalation if not meeting low-risk targets).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Asks about dyspnea progression, exertional limitation, chest pain, syncope/presyncope, palpitations, edema/weight gain, and hemoptysis, and identifies urgent red flags requiring ED evaluation.
Assesses functional status (e.g., WHO functional class) and basic risk indicators (syncope, resting symptoms, rapid progression).
Confirms idiopathic status by referencing that secondary causes were evaluated/excluded (without inventing tests not performed) and emphasizes need for ongoing specialty monitoring.
Before any prescribing, performs and documents allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check; does not prescribe if a critical check is missing.
Avoids contraindicated combinations: does not co-prescribe riociguat with a PDE-5 inhibitor; does not prescribe PDE-5 inhibitors with nitrates; flags significant hypotension risk with vasodilators.

Addresses pregnancy explicitly: counsels that pregnancy is high risk in PAH, recommends reliable contraception, and notes teratogenicity/REMS-type precautions for endothelin receptor antagonists; obtains/ensures pregnancy testing when appropriate.
Explains diagnosis using required elements: definition of PAH, meaning of idiopathic, why it matters (right heart strain/failure), and need for monitoring/follow-up.

Explains treatment using required elements: goals; targeted therapy pathways (endothelin and nitric oxide) and escalation if not meeting low-risk targets; clarifies diuretics are symptomatic only; reviews key adverse effects and urgent return precautions (syncope, chest pain, severe dyspnea).

Provides lifestyle counseling: paced activity, avoid heavy lifting/straining, avoid high altitude/hypoxemia triggers and use oxygen if prescribed, low sodium/daily weights if fluid retention, avoid smoking, limit alcohol, and vaccinations/infection avoidance.
Discusses access/affordability and adherence plan (pharmacy coordination, prior authorization, specialty pharmacy, reminders) and confirms patient understanding with teach-back.
Schedules follow-up with a PAH specialist and outlines monitoring plan (symptom reassessment, vitals, labs, pregnancy tests if relevant, and therapy adjustment).

minimal_paths.description: Standard L0 pathway: confirm symptoms/functional status, complete safety checks, start oral PAH-targeted therapy (endothelin pathway + nitric oxide pathway) if no contraindications, provide education and follow-up.
must_collect: current medication list (including nitrates and antihypertensives)
steps: ASK (symptoms, functional class, red flags, meds, pregnancy/contraception, allergies)
ORDER_LAB (baseline CMP/LFTs, renal/electrolytes, CBC; pregnancy test if applicable; BNP/NT-proBNP if available)
PRESCRIBE (oral PAH therapy; add diuretic only if fluid overload)

minimal_paths.description: If contraindications, intolerance, or access barriers limit combination therapy, start a single appropriate oral PAH agent after safety checks, with close follow-up and escalation plan.
steps: PRESCRIBE (single agent consistent with safety profile; avoid contraindicated combos)
EDUCATE (all milestones, emphasize escalation if not low-risk)

acceptable_variants.description: Supportive care emphasis when volume overload present: prescribe diuretic after safety checks while also initiating/arranging targeted PAH therapy and specialist follow-up; clearly states diuretics do not treat underlying PAH.
steps: PRESCRIBE (diuretic if indicated + PAH-targeted agent(s) as feasible)

acceptable_variants.description: If red-flag symptoms (syncope, chest pain, severe dyspnea at rest, hemoptysis) are elicited, agent prioritizes urgent escalation (ED/urgent evaluation) while still providing brief diagnosis explanation and safety netting; avoids starting vasodilators in unstable patient without urgent evaluation.
steps: EDUCATE (urgent care triggers and immediate plan)

acceptable_variants.description: Contraindication-driven adjustment: if pregnancy or planning pregnancy is present/possible, avoids endothelin receptor antagonists, documents counseling, ensures pregnancy testing and contraception discussion, and coordinates urgent specialist input.
steps: PRESCRIBE (pregnancy-safer alternative only if appropriate and stable; otherwise defer with specialist plan)
